orchestration_core.overview: LangGraph is the orchestration layer for this system. StateGraph replaces the custom AgentRegistry, SupervisorRouter, and PipelineOrchestrator. MemorySaver (or SqliteSaver/RedisSaver) replaces SessionStore. The compiled graph IS the orchestrator — it is built once in FastAPI lifespan and stored on app.state.graph. Routes call graph.ainvoke() with a thread_id for session scoping. For individual node and tool implementations, see 3_agent_design.xml.

docs_reference.mcp_note: Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.

state_types.description: State is a TypedDict that all nodes share. MessagesState is the built-in base — it provides a "messages" key with an add_messages reducer (appends instead of overwrites). Extend it only when routing metadata or additional fields are needed beyond messages.
state_types.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (verify all imports and field definitions via MCP): - MessagesState is the base — import path and internal field structure must come from MCP - SupervisorState extends MessagesState with a routing field (e.g., next_agent: str) set by the supervisor node and read by the routing function - RoutedState extends MessagesState with a classification field (e.g., route_label: str) set by a classifier node and read by the conditional edge function - For parallel fan-out: use Annotated with a list reducer — verify reducer import via MCP Only define the state extensions your topology actually requires. File: backend/app/graph/state.py

supervisor_pattern.description: One supervisor node reads the conversation and decides which worker runs next. Workers are either create_react_agent subgraphs or custom node functions. Workers route back to the supervisor to enable multi-hop chaining. When no more work is needed, the supervisor routes to END.
supervisor_pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (all method names must be verified via MCP): 1. supervisor_node: async function, calls LLM once at temperature=0.0, parses response to extract routing decision, returns {"next_agent": decision} 2. route_after_supervisor: pure function, reads next_agent from state, returns worker name string or END — unknown values fall through to END (safety net) 3. build_graph(): - StateGraph(SupervisorState) — verify StateGraph import via MCP - add_node("supervisor", supervisor_node) - add_node("worker_x", create_worker_x()) — one per worker from implementation.md - add_edge(START, "supervisor") — verify START import via MCP - add_conditional_edges("supervisor", route_after_supervisor, routing_map) - add_edge("worker_x", "supervisor") — workers loop back for multi-hop chaining - compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver()) — verify MemorySaver import via MCP 4. WORKER_AGENTS list must exactly match all node names added to builder — keep in sync 5. Supervisor system prompt must name EXACT values the LLM should return — no ambiguity

notes.note: WORKER_AGENTS list and the conditional_edges map must stay in sync — add both when adding a worker
MemorySaver is in-memory: sessions are lost on process restart — acceptable for dev, not production
Workers returning to supervisor enables multi-hop: supervisor can chain multiple workers per request

pipeline_pattern.description: For fixed sequential workflows: linear edges between nodes, no supervisor needed. Use when steps have strict dependencies and the sequence never changes at runtime.
pipeline_pattern.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Structural pattern (verify all method names and import paths via MCP): - StateGraph([state_type]) — use MessagesState unless stages need additional fields - add_node for each stage in sequence - add_edge(START, "stage_a") - add_edge("stage_a", "stage_b") - ... one add_edge per stage transition ... - add_edge("last_stage", END) - compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver()) No conditional edges needed — pipeline never branches at runtime. File: backend/app/graph/builder.py

session_management.description: LangGraph's checkpointer handles session state automatically. Pass thread_id in the config dict to scope memory to a session. No separate SessionStore class is needed — MemorySaver IS the session store.
session_management.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Pattern: Pass config={"configurable": {"thread_id": session_id}} on every ainvoke call. Verify: the exact key names ("configurable", "thread_id") via MCP — these are the LangGraph session scoping contract. Without thread_id, all users share one conversation. Return: access the final response from the result messages list — verify result structure via MCP.
session_management.production_note: MemorySaver stores state in process memory — lost on restart, not multi-process safe. For production, replace with a persistent checkpointer: Query MCP: search_docs_by_lang_chain("LangGraph SqliteSaver PostgresSaver persistent checkpointer current") - SqliteSaver: single-server persistence (no extra infra) - PostgresSaver: multi-server / cloud deployment See also: 7_best_practices.xml production_hardening section.

fastapi_integration.description: Build the graph once in the FastAPI lifespan and store it on app.state. Routes retrieve it via request.app.state.graph — no global state, no DI container.
fastapi_integration.implementation_guidance:
The lifespan function in main.py must perform in this order: 1. Check Ollama is reachable via httpx GET to OLLAMA_BASE_URL — log warning if not, do not crash 2. Warm up the default model — send a minimal test invocation to prevent cold-start on first request Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query. Use MCP-confirmed message class and invoke method names for the warm-up call 3. Build and store the compiled graph: app.state.graph = build_graph() 4. Log a confirmation at each step
See 8_code_generation.xml fastapi_app_template for the stable FastAPI boilerplate structure. The LangChain/LangGraph specific calls (warm-up, graph invocation) must use MCP-verified syntax.

error_handling.description: Node functions should not raise unhandled exceptions. Wrap risky operations in try/except and return error messages so the graph continues routing.
error_handling.implementation_guidance:
Phase 0 Step 4 covers this — use confirmed results, do not re-query.
Pattern: Every node that calls the LLM or external services must: - Wrap the entire body in try/except Exception - On success: return {"messages": [AIMessage(content=result)]} - On error: log with logger.error(), return {"messages": [AIMessage(content=f"Error: {str(e)}")]} - Never let an exception propagate out of a node — the graph will crash
Verify: AIMessage import path and constructor signature via MCP before implementing.

convention.pattern: snake_case — verb or domain_role: supervisor_node, summarise_node, research_agent
convention.note: create_react_agent factory functions named create_X_agent(); node name passed to add_node() is the graph edge label

convention.pattern: build_X_graph() factory functions; compiled result stored in app.state.graph
